Title: Johor RTD records over 70,000 traffic offences in first four months of 2026
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Johor Road Transport Department (RTD) recorded 71,320 offences under the Road Transport Act 1987 during operations in the first four months of this year.
State director Zulkarnain Yasin said the highest number of offences involved expired Motor Vehicle Licences (LKM), with 11,970 cases, or 15 per cent.
"This was followed by offences involving vehicles without insurance, with 9,983 cases (14 per cent), driving without a Competent Driving Licence (CDL), with 1,216 cases (2 per cent), and driving without a vocational licence (2 per cent).
"During the same period, we also conducted 341,917 vehicle inspections, of which action was taken against 40,539 vehicles," he said at a press conference after Ops 1J at the Jalan Skudai-Pontian interchange here last night.
Zulkarnain said RTD also recorded 10,287 cases or 14 per cent involving nine major offences, including failure to wear seat belts, not wearing helmets, overloading and failing to comply with traffic lights.
Meanwhile, 15,715 technical offences were recorded, including those involving registration numbers, tinted windows, vehicle modifications and retreaded tyres.
Commenting on the operation, which began at 8pm, Zulkarnain said 9,356 vehicles were inspected.
"Of the total, 2,824 offences were recorded and action was taken against 1,896 vehicles for various offences.
"We will continue similar operations, including at other locations throughout the state, to ensure compliance with road laws among road users."
During the same operation, 11 foreigners comprising seven Myanmar nationals, three Bangladeshis and one Indonesian, were detained for offences under the Immigration Act.
A total of 304 enforcement personnel from various agencies were involved in the two-hour integrated operation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Schools must be safe havens for our children
Article:
LETTERS:
Recent incidents involving students have raised serious concern among parents, educators and the public about safety in schools.
Schools are meant to be places where children can learn, grow, and thrive in a safe and protected environment.
Any incident that threatens the safety and well-being of students must therefore be treated with utmost seriousness and urgency.
In this regard, the Education Ministry's move to finalise new guidelines aimed at strengthening safety in schools nationwide is timely and commendable.
The initiative, developed with input from the Education Institution Safety Reform Committee, reflects the growing awareness that stronger and more comprehensive safety measures are urgently needed following a series of unfortunate accidents involving students.
The recent case involving a school van which knocked down a primary school student at the school gate is deeply disturbing and should serve as a wake-up call to all parties.
Such incidents should never happen within school compounds or surrounding areas where children are present.
It is therefore high time for the Education Ministry to undertake a comprehensive review of all existing school safety guidelines, protocols, and enforcement mechanisms to ensure that similar tragedies do not recur.
Equally crucial is the need to cultivate a strong culture of safety in every school. In this regard, there is an urgent need to actively introduce and implement Occupational Safety and Health (OSH) programmes in schools nationwide.
Schools are also workplaces and must comply with the requirements and spirit of the Occupational Safety and Health Act 1994.
Every school should establish an Occupational Safety and Health Committee comprising school administrators, teachers, staff representatives, and where appropriate, parent representatives.
Such committees can play an important role in identifying safety hazards, conducting regular risk assessments, monitoring compliance, and recommending improvements to enhance safety standards within school premises.
Among the areas that require immediate attention are traffic management within school compounds, pedestrian safety at school gates, emergency response procedures, fire safety, building maintenance, electrical safety, sports and playground safety, laboratory safety, food hygiene, and mental health support systems for students and teachers.
Regular safety audits should also be conducted in all schools, while safety awareness and preparedness programmes must become an integral part of school culture.
Teachers, students, bus and van operators, canteen workers, and contractors entering school premises should all be educated and trained on safety responsibilities and procedures.
The safety of our children must never be compromised.
Preventive action is always better than dealing with tragedies after they occur.
Every child has the right to study in a safe environment, and every parent has the right to send their children to school with peace of mind.
Let us therefore work together — the government, schools, parents, communities, and all stakeholders — to make safety a top priority in every school across the nation.
TAN SRI LEE LAM THYE
Safety Advocate
Kuala Lumpur
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Army veteran injured after car overturns in three-vehicle crash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: An 84-year-old army veteran sustained minor injuries when his car overturned after crashing into two parked vehicles at Jalan 34/26, Wangsa Maju, on Thursday.
City Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa said the incident occurred at 3.15pm while the man was driving his car from Jelatek towards Sri Rampai.
"Initial investigations found the car is believed to have lost control before crashing into a Toyota Corolla Cross and a Proton Saga parked on the left shoulder of the road," he said in a statement.
Zamzuri said the driver suffered minor injuries and received outpatient treatment at Kuala Lumpur Hospital.
"The driver of the Toyota Corolla Cross, a 36-year-old Arab man who was inside the vehicle at the time, and the 28-year-old owner of the Proton Saga were not unhurt."
Preliminary investigations suggest the accident was caused by a health issue, as the senior citizen reportedly experienced sudden bladder pain and lost control of the vehicle.
The case is being investigated under Section 43(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
"Members of the public with information or who witnessed the incident can contact the Tun H.S. Lee Traffic Police Station at 03-2071 9999 or the nearest police station to assist investigations."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Raging fire at Mexican fairground kills five
Article:
MEXICO CITY: At least five people died in a fire at a fairground in the southeastern Mexican city of Villahermosa on Thursday, authorities said, after the massive blaze broke out during a concert attended by thousands.
Authorities do not know what caused the fire, which erupted early on Thursday morning and is now under control, Civil Protection officials said.
Media outlets and social media users shared images showing dozens of people fleeing the scene as a column of black smoke enveloped the sky.
Tabasco state Governor Javier May expressed his condolences to the families of the victims, who he said included fair exhibitors unable to leave the premises in time.
May added in a message on X that investigations have begun to determine the fire's cause and announced an economic recovery program for affected merchants and businesses.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Countries track passengers of virus-hit cruise ship
Article:
AMSTERDAM/GENEVA: Countries worldwide sought to prevent further spread of the hantavirus on Thursday, after an outbreak on a cruise ship, by tracking those who had disembarked before the virus was detected and anyone in close contact with them since.
Three people – a Dutch couple and a German national – died in the outbreak on the MV Hondius.
In total, five people are confirmed to have contracted the virus, with another three suspected cases, the World Health Organization said. Hantavirus is usually spread by rodents but can in rare cases be transmitted person-to-person.
All passengers who disembarked in St Helena in the South Atlantic Ocean, where the ship made a stop on April 24, have been contacted, the ship's operator said. This included people from at least 12 countries, among them seven British citizens and six from the US. The first confirmed case of hantavirus in this outbreak came in early May.
THIS IS NOT COVID
The WHO repeated that the risk to the general public was low even if the Andean strain of the virus, found in several victims, can in rare cases be transmitted among humans.
"This is not coronavirus, this is a very different virus," Maria Van Kerkhove, WHO director of epidemic and pandemic management, told a press conference. "This is not the same situation we were in six years ago." The WHO said it was working on step-by-step guidance for when the dozens of passengers remaining on the ship, which is sailing to the Canary Islands, arrives there on Saturday or Sunday and the passengers disembark and travel home. None of these passengers currently have any symptoms.
CONTACT TRACING, MONITORING
The United States' Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said it was closely monitoring the situation, adding that the risk to the American public was extremely low at the time.
US President Donald Trump told reporters on Thursday he had been briefed on the hantavirus and expressed hope that it was under control.
"It's very much, we hope, under control," Trump said.
Asked whether Americans should be concerned about any spread of the virus, Trump replied: "I hope not." He also said, without elaborating, that a report on the virus was expected on Friday.
[[nid:1434772]]
The Georgia Department of Public Health said it was monitoring two asymptomatic residents who had returned home after disembarking from the cruise ship. The Arizona Department of Health Services said it was monitoring one resident, who was also on the ship, and was not symptomatic. According to the New York Times, California was monitoring a number of residents who had been on the ship.
And in Texas, officials said that two residents who were passengers on the ship returned to the US before the outbreak was identified.
One French citizen has been in contact with a person who had fallen ill but was not showing symptoms, officials said.
CONTACT TRACING
Oceanwide Expeditions said it was now working to establish details of all passengers and crew who embarked and disembarked on various stops since March 20.
The Dutch couple who died, and who are believed to be the first hantavirus cases of this outbreak, boarded on April 1.
Dutch airline KLM said it had taken the Dutch woman off a plane in Johannesburg on April 25 due to her deteriorating medical condition. She died before she could reach the Netherlands.
According to broadcaster RTL, a KLM stewardess who had been in contact with her has been admitted to a hospital in Amsterdam after showing possible hantavirus symptoms.
Crew and passengers who helped the Dutch woman who passed away are being called daily for health checks, Dutch authorities told public broadcaster NOS.
EVACUATIONS, TESTS
Three patients were evacuated from the ship on Wednesday. Two have been admitted to a hospital in the Netherlands, while another was transferred to Germany for medical care.
Martin Anstee, an expedition guide, was one of the two evacuees in hospital in the Netherlands, according to Sky News, and told them he was "doing okay" but "there are still lots of tests to be done."
The Duesseldorf University Clinic, treating the German evacuee, said she was not a confirmed case but rather a contact and was undergoing tests.
In Switzerland, a man who travelled on the cruise ship and was admitted to a hospital tested positive for the infection, officials said.
A Danish citizen who was aboard the Hondius has returned home and has been advised to self-isolate as a precaution, Danish health authorities said.
In Canada, officials said that two Canadians on the cruise returned home before the outbreak was identified and another Canadian was on the same flight as a symptomatic person. All three are currently asymptomatic.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Estee Lauder reaches US$210 million settlement over China sales practices
Article:
NEW YORK: Estee Lauder has reached a US$210 million settlement of a lawsuit accusing the cosmetic giant of defrauding shareholders by concealing its overdependence on improper gray-market sales in China.
A preliminary all-cash settlement of the proposed class action was filed on Thursday in Manhattan federal court, and requires approval by US District Judge Arun Subramanian.
The case concerned a practice known as "daigou," in which resellers buy luxury goods at low duty-free prices and resell the goods at below-market prices to consumers.
Shareholders said the company was heavily dependent on daigou, especially in the Hainan province, after the Covid pandemic began, and waited too long to reveal how a January 2022 Chinese government crackdown on the practice was hurting sales.
They said the New York-based company concealed the truth until Nov 1, 2023, causing its shares to plunge 19 per cent and wiping out about US$8.7 billion of market value.
Estee generates about one-fifth of its sales in mainland China.
The company denied wrongdoing in agreeing to settle, and said insurance would cover some of the settlement costs.
Estee did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Subramanian had rejected Estee's bid to dismiss the lawsuit in March 2025, faulting the company for touting reasons for its success "while leaving out the parts of the truth it found inconvenient."
The shareholders are led by three Michigan public pension funds. Their lawyers plan to seek up to 32 per cent of the settlement fund, or US$67.2 million, in fees, court papers show.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 10 Perak villages picked for community-led drug-free initiative
Article:
IPOH: Ten villages across Perak have been selected for the Drug-Free Aspirational Village (KABD) initiative to empower communities to lead the fight against substance abuse.
Perak National Anti-Drugs Agency (Nada) director Mohamad Nor Musa said the initiative, introduced through the District Drug Eradication Action Council, involved one village from each district in the state.
Among those selected are Ulu Kuang in Kinta and Kampung Senggang in Kuala Kangsar.
Mohamad said the programme was designed to encourage communities to identify at-risk persons and persuade them to seek treatment voluntarily.
"We want the community to drive these efforts. Previously, people relied only on complaints and reports, but these problems originate within families and communities.
"We want communities empowered to help the government tackle these issues," he said after a media appreciation night here last night.
Villages were selected based on the presence of addicts or people at risk of drug abuse (OYDS), alongside the commitment of community leaders.
"We choose villages with strong commitment because when a programme succeeds, we want other villages to use it as a model," he said.
Mohamad said Nada hoped to expand the initiative by adding another 10 villages annually until 2030.
The agency is also working to change public perception of addicts by encouraging families to treat them with compassion rather than isolation.
"Previously, people tended to ridicule and isolate addicts, which only pushed them away and made them more rebellious.
"If we recognise them as patients who need care, we can change this approach," he said.
The programme, which began in January, is currently in the profiling stage to identify individuals requiring intervention.
Outreach visits and family engagement sessions are also being conducted to encourage voluntary treatment.
"This is a far more positive approach than simply arresting people. We want communities to feel responsible for their fellow villagers," he said.
On rehabilitation, Mohamad said Nada now offered flexible voluntary treatment packages ranging from three months to two years, depending on the severity of the addiction.
"Unlike the previous mandatory two-year approach, the voluntary programme offers shorter packages based on screening and assessments.
"Clients and assessors can jointly determine the appropriate duration," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil jumps and stock futures slip on renewed US-Iran fighting
Article:
SINGAPORE: Oil rose and US stock futures slipped in early trade on Friday, after the United States and Iran exchanged fire and put a month-long Middle East ceasefire in doubt.
US crude futures, which are lower for the week, jumped more than two per cent from Thursday's close to US$96.8 a barrel.
S&P 500 futures slipped about 0.2 per cent and Nikkei futures pointed to a slightly lower open for Japanese shares , which soared to record highs on Thursday.
Iran's top joint military command said the US targeted an Iranian oil tanker and another ship entering the Strait of Hormuz.
The US military said it acted in self-defence after Iran attacked Navy destroyers that were passing through the strait. It said Iran did not hit any US assets in the attack and US President Donald Trump told ABC News that the ceasefire with Iran remained in place.
The strikes occurred while Washington awaited Iran's response to a US proposal that would halt fighting but leave the most contentious issues, such as Iran's nuclear programme, unresolved for now.
In foreign exchange markets, the rising tension lifted the dollar off its recent lows and set it on course to finish the week mostly steady.
The yen has struggled to strengthen past 155 to the dollar, despite data suggesting that authorities in Tokyo may have sold as much as $67 billion in defence of the currency this week and last.
The yen last traded at 156.88 per dollar and the euro at US$1.1726. US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is due to visit Tokyo next week and markets will be on alert for any comments he might make on the yen or Japan's monetary policy, given his past remarks in favour of speedier Japanese rate hikes.
Investors await the US non-farm payrolls report on Friday, with jobs expected to have increased in April by 62,000 after rebounding 178,000 in March, a Reuters survey of economists shows.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nada: Students as young as 11 in Perak using vapes, synthetic drugs
Article:
IPOH: The involvement of students as young as 11 in vape and synthetic drug abuse has become a worrying trend in Perak.
Perak National Anti-Drugs Agency (Nada) director Mohamad Nor Musa said the use of vapes mixed with prohibited substances, synthetic liquids, and hallucinogenic substances such as magic mushrooms was increasing due to easy accessibility and social media promotion.
"Teenagers and students are among the highest-risk groups due to peer influence, social media culture and lack of awareness regarding the actual contents of the substances used.
"Abuse of these substances can affect academic performance, discipline, mental health, and students' futures if not curbed early," he told reporters after hosting Media Appreciation Night with Perak media practitioners here last night.
Mohamad said while there were no specific statistics yet on student involvement, reports indicated an increase in underage students abusing vape products and synthetic substances.
He said many substances used today were categorised as New Psychoactive Substances (NPS), including fentanyl, magic mushrooms, synthetic opioids, and synthetic cannabinoids, which were difficult to detect through conventional screening.
"Standard tests often fail to detect them. Samples must be sent for laboratory analysis, and if they are not detected at the screening stage, authorities cannot detain the individuals because the tests come back negative.
"This is indeed a major challenge for enforcement officers, including Nada," he said.
Mohamad said drug abuse continued to pose a serious threat to individuals, family institutions, community wellbeing, public safety, and national development.
National statistics as of December 2025 showed 191,083 individuals were involved in drug and substance abuse, with 14,915 cases recorded in Perak.
"Although this marked a 6.79 per cent decrease compared to 2024, the figure remains at a level that requires continuous action from all stakeholders," he said.
He added that current trends showed abuse largely involved synthetic drugs, particularly stimulant-type ATS drugs.
In Perak, 5,561 people aged between 15 and 39 were recorded as ATS users, while the use of synthetic drugs increased by 39.9 per cent compared with 2023, he said.
Mohamad said relapse and rehabilitation remained major obstacles, with contributing factors including environmental influence, life pressures, lack of family support, and social stigma.
He said this showed preventive approaches alone were insufficient and required a comprehensive strategy involving treatment, early intervention, and employment opportunities.
He also welcomed the Perak government's move to ban vape use beginning January this year and expressed support for measures introduced by the state Health Department to address substance abuse from both health and preventive perspectives.
On proposals to implement drug screening in schools, Mohamad said Nada believed a balanced and practical approach should be prioritised.
"The main focus is not to punish students but to assist them through targeted screening, education, prevention, counselling and psychosocial support.
"The involvement of parents, schools, communities and relevant agencies is crucial to ensure students do not continue to fall into the trap of substance abuse," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens higher against major currencies, eases versus greenback
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened higher against most major currencies but eased versus the US dollar as investors remained cautious following renewed escalation in the West Asia conflict, which dimmed hopes for a peace deal between the United States and Iran.
At 8am, the local unit traded at 3.9100/9200 against the greenback, compared with Thursday's close of 3.9070/9115.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the US Dollar Index is quite flat, at around 98 points, as market participants await the release of the latest US jobs data.
Meanwhile, he noted that the ringgit closed 0.42 per cent higher against the greenback to RM3.9093 yesterday, after Bank Negara Malaysia kept the overnight policy rate (OPR) unchanged at 2.75 per cent.
"They remain confident that strong economic fundamentals and sound economic policies would help the country's economy to sustain its growth momentum.
"Hence, steady OPR should be ringgit-positive. On that note, expect the local currency to remain around RM3.90 against the greenback today," he told Bernama.
At the opening, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies.
It strengthened against the euro to 4.5872/5989 from 4.5978/6031at Thursday's close, rose versus the British pound to 5.3004/3140 from 5.3202/3263 previously, and strengthened against the Japanese yen to 2.4925/4990 from 2.4982/5013 from yesterday.
The local currency was mostly higher against regional peers.
It appreciated against the Singapore dollar to 3.0807/0890 from 3.0861/0901at Thursday's close and strengthened versus the Thai baht to 12.1098/1479 from 12.1486/1687.
The local note traded almost flat against the Indonesian rupiah at 225.5/226.2 from 225.4/225, and there was little change versus the Philippine peso at 6.47/4/6.49 from 6.46/6.48.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police rule out bullying in student's fall from school garage roof
Article:
MELAKA: Police have dismissed claims that a Year Five student who fell from a school garage roof in Batu Berendam was a victim of bullying.
Melaka Tengah police chief Assistant Commissioner Christopher Patit said initial investigations into the Tuesday incident revealed the boy had thrown another student's shoes onto the roof.
He then climbed onto the garage structure to retrieve them.
"The roof collapsed as the boy attempted to climb back up to the first floor of the school building. He fell and injured his right leg," Christopher said in a statement.
The student received first aid at the school before being sent to Melaka Hospital. He was later transferred to Putra Specialist Hospital for further treatment.
"The student is reported to be in stable condition with a minor fracture to his right ankle," he said.
Christopher said further investigations by the criminal investigation department, the forensics team, and witness statements confirmed the fall was an accident.
"The victim also confirmed he was not bullied, abused, or neglected before or during the incident," he said.
Earlier, an image of an ambulance at the school circulated on WhatsApp, alongside claims that a Year Five student had fallen from the building.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stocks pull back, oil choppy as US-Iran peace deal remains in flux
Article:
NEW YORK: US and European stocks slipped on Thursday, as oil prices swung between gains and losses in volatile trading with investors still uncertain about peace negotiations between the US and Iran.
On Wall Street, major stock indexes pulled back slightly a day after strong chipmaker earnings pushed them to multiple records. The S&P 500 fell 0.4 per cent, the Nasdaq Composite dipped 0.1 per cent and the Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 0.6 per cent.
Oil prices ultimately settled lower after a report said Saudi Arabia and Kuwait lifted restrictions on US use of their airspace and military bases, allowing Washington to restart operations to escort commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz as early as this week.
Brent crude futures settled down 1.2 per cent or US$1.21 at US$100.06 a barrel, while US West Texas Intermediate crude futures settled down 0.28 per cent or 27 cents at US$94.81. Both benchmarks had earlier declined by as much as US$5 a barrel on optimism that Washington and Tehran were moving toward a limited, temporary agreement to halt their conflict.
Europe's STOXX 600 finished 1.1 per cent lower, having jumped 2.2 per cent on Wednesday, while MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan hit a fresh all-time high, up 1.6 per cent. Japan's Nikkei crossed 62,000 for the first time.
MSCI's All-Country World Index ticked down 0.1 per cent, holding around record highs.
OIL RISK
"Oil volatility may be having less of an effect on the stock market's day-to-day performance, but its longer-term impact on inflation is still an open question," Daniel Skelly, head of Morgan Stanley's Wealth Management Market Research & Strategy Team, wrote in a note on Thursday.
Brent is still around 40 per cent above its late-February level, when the war began, while 10-year Treasury yields have surged - a reminder of the strain higher energy costs continue to put on the global economy. Ten-year US Treasury yields rose by 2.8 basis points to 4.382 per cent.
"Certainly the clock is ticking towards a point ... when the pace at which oil inventory drawdowns at the current pace become unsustainable and energy prices jump materially," Investec market strategists wrote in a note on Thursday.
Rocketing oil prices whacked global markets in March but a fragile ceasefire and prospect of a deal have spurred a risk-on rally since April that has been fueled by strong tech earnings reports.
S&P COMPANIES SET FOR ROBUST PROFIT GROWTH
S&P 500 companies are on track for their strongest profit growth in more than four years, although Intel and Advanced Micro Devices declined on Thursday, paring gains from earlier this week.
"US earnings confirm a broad-based profit boom - record EPS (earnings per share) beats, all-time-high margins and sharply upgraded '26 growth expectations," Manish Kabra, a market strategist with Societe Generale, wrote in a client note on Thursday.
Investors await the US non-farm payrolls report on Friday, with jobs expected to have increased in April by 62,000 after rebounding 178,000 in March, a Reuters survey of economists shows.
In currency markets, the euro was little changed at US$1.174. The dollar index, which measures the US currency against six units, was also flat.
The yen remained in the spotlight after spikes in recent sessions prompted market speculation that Japan had intervened to support the long-battered currency.
The yen ticked down 0.24 per cent at 156.76 per dollar, having hit a 10-week high of 155 on Wednesday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: OCBC Q1 profit beats forecasts, sets aside more allowances amid Middle East war
Article:
SINGAPORE: Singapore's Oversea-Chinese Banking Corp (OCBC) on Friday flagged heightened macro risks from the war in the Middle East, after posting a five per cent rise in first-quarter net profit driven by growth in its wealth business.
OCBC, the country's second-largest bank, reported a five per cent year-on-year rise in its net profit for the January-March period, bolstered by non-interest income which grew more than 20 per cent from the year ago period.
However, the lender set aside S$191 million (US$150.51 million) of allowances for non-impaired assets, including additional precautionary buffers to reflect elevated uncertainties in the operating environment, it said in slides accompanying its results.
That compares with S$118 million from the previous year, and a S$36 million write back in the fourth quarter of 2025.
"Looking ahead, global conditions remain uncertain amid geopolitical tensions and elevated inflation risks," said OCBC's Group CEO Tan Teck Long.
"Much of the near-term outlook will depend on how the war in the Middle East, with its impact on energy supply and prices, evolves, while the ongoing trade tariff situation is also being closely monitored."
For the quarter, net profit of Southeast Asia's second-largest lender by assets climbed to S$1.97 billion (US$1.55 billion) from S$1.88 billion a year earlier.
This beat the mean estimate of around S$1.89 billion from three analysts surveyed by LSEG.
Net interest margin, a key profitability gauge, fell to 1.76 per cent during the quarter from 2.04 per cent a year earlier.
The lender's fee income for the quarter jumped 24 per cent on-year, led by wealth management fees which rose an annual 34 per cent to S$422 million.
OCBC, which counts Singapore, greater China, Indonesia and Malaysia among its key markets, maintained 2026 outlook guidance.
Its results rounded up a resilient first quarter earnings season for Singapore banks, with wealth and fee income helping cushion pressure from lower interest rates and an uncertain global backdrop.
Larger peer DBS Group last week reported stronger first-quarter earnings that beat analysts' forecasts, while smaller rival United Overseas Bank on Thursday posted better-than-expected but weaker results.
Earlier this week, OCBC announced it has agreed to buy certain assets and liabilities of HSBC's wealth and premier banking portfolio in Indonesia, marking the first major deal under Tan since he took over in January.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IS-linked Australian women charged with keeping slave in Syria
Article:
SYDNEY: Two Australian women "kept a female slave" after travelling to Syria in 2014 to support the Islamic State group, police said Friday after the pair were charged in Melbourne.
The pair returned to Australia on Thursday evening for the first time in almost a decade, travelling from a Syrian detention camp where they were stranded after the group's collapse.
They were immediately arrested after their Qatar Airways flight landed at Melbourne International Airport.
Police accused the women – a mother and daughter aged 53 and 31 – of "crimes against humanity" while living under Islamic State's self-declared caliphate.
The 53-year-old woman was "complicit in the purchase of a female slave for US$10,000," the Australian Federal Police said.
The 31-year-old woman had "knowingly kept a female slave in the home."
Hundreds of women from Western nations were lured to the Middle East as the Islamic State group gained prominence in the early 2010s, in many cases following husbands who had signed up as jihadist fighters.
Australia, Canada, the United Kingdom and others are still grappling with how to treat citizens stranded after the group collapsed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Circular on working hours for junior doctors must be accompanied by strict enforcement [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hartal Doktor Kontrak has called for strict enforcement and independent monitoring over the Health Ministry's proposed circular on junior doctors' working hours.
Its spokesman said a clear oversight mechanism was needed to ensure compliance at both hospital and department levels.
"The circular must come with strict implementation and oversight. Those who fail to comply must answer and be reprimanded strictly," the spokesman told the New Straits Times.
The spokesman also proposed that the ministry appoint independent auditors to assess whether hospitals were adhering to the regulations.
"An independent auditor should come to review and check if the rules are being followed strictly.
"For departments that do not comply, the department head should be answerable."
The spokesman was responding to a statement from Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad that the ministry will soon issue a new circular on
working hours
for junior medical officers to strengthen governance and address concerns over excessive workloads among house officers.
He had said the circular would reinforce existing flexi-working arrangements for house officers following reports that some were working between 65 and 85 hours a week.
Malaysian Medics International had highlighted the heavy workloads faced by junior doctors in Malaysia, saying they were significantly higher than in other countries.
House officers or medical officers in Malaysia reportedly work an average of 65 to 85 hours or more a week, far higher than their counterparts in the United Kingdom, who work 40 hours, and Ireland, where the average is 48 hours.
Meanwhile, a second-year house officer who wished to be known only as Nana described the workload as "crazy and inhumane", claiming that actual working hours often far exceeded official schedules.
She said although official working hours were from 7am to 8pm, junior doctors in her department typically worked from 6am to 10pm.
Nana added that post-call duties sometimes could stretch for 17 hours, from 7pm until noon the following day, as housemen were required to complete patient discharge duties.
Asked about the ministry's plan to introduce the circular, she expressed scepticism over whether it would be enforced effectively on the ground.
"The government can say anything, but hospitals won't enforce it.
"I think everywhere in Malaysia is understaffed… Around 30 per cent of medical graduates from my university batch of 180 had opted to pursue housemanship in Singapore instead," she said.
When asked how she was coping mentally and physically, she replied: "Badly and badly. But we just close our eyes and go through it.
"I rely on energy drinks just to get through the day," she said.
Asked whether she had taken medical leave this year, she said she had only done so once after expressing suicidal thoughts.
She said she was later given a week's medical leave by the psychiatry department, which she claimed was linked to the long working hours and heavy workload.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM43.5m approved to fast-track 358 projects
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI: The Johor government has approved RM43.5 million to fast-track 358 grassroots projects aimed at addressing the immediate needs of the people.
The projects are being managed under the Mesyuarat Tindakan DUN (MTD) mechanism, a state-level coordination platform designed to cut red tape and accelerate the resolution of local issues.
MTD also ensures faster coordination and delivery of basic infrastructure projects across Johor constituencies.
Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said the MTD allowed agencies and decision-makers to converge and break down siloed working, ensuring faster delivery of basic infrastructure and community facilities.
Speaking during his winding-up speech at the state assembly in Bangunan Sultan Ismail yesterday, Onn Hafiz said the approvals reflected the government's commitment to resolving issues efficiently at the ground level.
"Through MTD, the state government's approach is clear. We do not just identify problems, but we work towards practical solutions within our capacity and jurisdiction.
"What can be resolved at state level, we will implement promptly."
[[nid:1434745]]
Of the 358 projects approved this year, 11 have been completed and 153 are under implementation. The remaining projects are in pre-implementation stages, including technical coordination on-site.
The projects cover essential community needs such as drainage upgrades, road and bridge repairs, streetlight installations, as well as public amenities and community infrastructure.
Onn Hafiz said MTD reflected Johor's dual focus on  large-scale development and everyday issues affecting the
rakyat
.
"Development is not only about mega projects.
"It is also about ensuring that daily problems faced by the people are addressed in a structured and consistent manner."
He also said that last year,  613 projects worth RM765 million were  implemented under the same mechanism  statewide.
Onn Hafiz expressed his appreciation to state executive councillors, district officers, assemblymen, local authorities as well as state and federal agencies for their cooperation in ensuring the effective
delivery of
rakyat
-focused initiatives.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Simpan SSPN 2025 dividend set at 4.10pc
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Simpan SSPN dividend for  last year has been set at 4.10 per cent, the highest rate recorded in the past 11 years.
The payout, involving an allocation of RM505.68 million, will benefit 6.07 million accounts.  Since its introduction in 2004, total dividend distribution  has reached RM2.63 billion.
National Higher Education Fund Corporation (PTPTN) chairman Datuk Seri Norliza Abdul Rahim said the increase from 4.05 per cent in 2024 reflected prudent fund management and consistent returns delivered to depositors.
"The higher rate underscores PTPTN's commitment to managing depositors' funds efficiently, guided by strong governance, integrity and strategic investment decisions to ensure optimal returns," she said on Wednesday.
She  added that the performance was supported by diversified investments in syariah-compliant bonds and Islamic bonds (sukuk) across short- and long-term money market instruments, aimed at preserving capital while delivering competitive returns.
"The increase in the dividend rate reflects PTPTN's effective management of savings funds and strategic investment portfolio selection."
The 4.10 per cent return also exceeded the overnight policy rate (OPR), which remained at 2.75 per cent throughout last year, highlighting the scheme's competitiveness as a savings instrument.
Up to  Dec 31, total deposits under Simpan SSPN stood at RM23.88 billion.Deposits recorded during the year amounted to RM3.33 billion, an increase of RM154.1 million or 4.86 per cent compared with RM3.17 billion in 2024.
More than 500,000 new accounts were opened last year, reflecting  public confidence in the scheme.
Norliza said the  growth demonstrated strong support and reaffirmed Simpan SSPN's role in encouraging early financial planning for higher education.
"Over the past two decades, Simpan SSPN has  recorded positive performance and continues to gain the confidence and trust of Malaysians.
"PTPTN has promoted a savings culture since 2004 through the introduction of Simpan SSPN,"
The dividend will be credited  into depositors' accounts, with statements accessible via the myPTPTN application from  yesterday.
Simpan SSPN also offers a range of benefits, including personal income tax relief of up to RM8,000 annually, free takaful protection and savings guaranteed by the government in line with syariah principles.
Under the 2025 Budget, eligibility has been expanded to include households earning between RM4,000 and RM6,000 under the Geran Padanan Ihsan  matching grant, allowing them to apply for grants of up to RM5,000 per family over two years.
Norliza said the competitive dividend rate was expected to encourage more people to start saving early for their children's tertiary education while motivating existing depositors to increase their contributions.
"Simpan SSPN remains a  secure and rewarding savings platform that continues to support families in planning for higher education expenses."
She expressed appreciation to depositors for their continued support and trust in the scheme, adding that PTPTN would continue strengthening its role as a trusted national education savings institution.
PTPTN is also organising the 2026 Simpan SSPN Draw Campaign from Jan 1 to Dec 31, offering prizes worth RM1.4 million to 954 winners.
More information can be obtained through the official portal at  www.ptptn.gov.my or via the myPTPTN application.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Push for 25pc tax revenue return to state
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI: All parties should uphold the decree by the Regent of Johor Tunku Mahkota Ismail Sultan Ibrahim for at least 25 per cent of the state's tax revenue to be returned to Johor.
Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said he was confident this could be realised through cooperation between the state and federal governments.
"I appreciate the support from several assemblymen who have also called for this tax return to be pursued.
"I am confident that with the presence of Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong, who is also  Perling assemblyman, in this house, this dream can be achieved," he said during his winding-up speech at the Johor state assembly   here yesterday.
[[nid:1434740]]
Onn Hafiz  said the state government was ready to ensure development projects  that have been announced  are expedited for the benefit of the people. Perhaps our counterparts at the federal level are busy managing the entire country.
"However, if the money is returned to the people of Johor to be managed and audited in detail to prevent leakages or waste, I am confident the state government can shoulder that responsibility."
He said several projects announced over the last two years had yet to receive funding. These include the Automated Rapid Transit (ART) system  to address traffic congestion once the Johor Baru-Singapore Rapid Transit System (RTS) Link is completed by the end of this year.
The delay has caused concern about traffic dispersal, especially in urban areas and national gateways. While the state has short-term plans to ease traffic flow, the people are still waiting for the ART project, he said.
Onn Hafiz also highlighted two other projects requiring urgent attention: the teaching hospital in Kota Tinggi and the Sultanah Aminah Hospital 2 (HSA 2) project.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Norway missile snag blunts LCS project [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Royal Malaysian Navy's (RMN) long-awaited littoral combat ships (LCS) were meant to mark a new chapter in Malaysia's maritime defence — sleek, modern warships equipped to project deterrence and safeguard national waters in an increasingly contested region.
However, fresh uncertainty surrounding the supply of their primary strike weapon, the Norwegian-made Naval Strike Missile (NSM), now threatens to blunt that ambition before the ships even enter service.
Defence analysts warn the setback could leave the navy's future frontline combatants operating without one of their most critical combat capabilities, raising wider questions over Malaysia's delayed naval modernisation efforts and its continued dependence on foreign defence suppliers.
Defence analyst Zaki Salleh said the absence of the missile capability could undermine the RMN's long-delayed modernisation plans under its "15-to-5" Transformation Programme.
"The RMN fleet will be handicapped without modern-generation strike vessels as outlined in its modernisation programme.
"This raises concerns over the navy's modernisation targets and, at the same time, the effectiveness of the country's maritime defence, which has already been delayed for years," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Zaki's comments follow the Norwegian government's decision to impose stricter controls on certain technologies, including the NSM, resulting in export approvals for the missiles being revoked some eight years after the supply contract with Malaysia was signed.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin on Wednesday said Malaysia will engage Norway through diplomatic channels to obtain further clarification and discuss appropriate measures to safeguard national interests.
Under the LCS programme, Malaysia was originally scheduled to receive its first vessel, based on the French Gowind-class design, in 2019.
Subsequent vessels were to enter service in phases to replace ageing surface combatants under the navy's modernisation roadmap.
However, the programme has faced prolonged delays, with the delivery schedule repeatedly revised over the years.
Even the first ship's commissioning  has now been pushed to December  due to late delivery of equipment and technical issues.
A TIGER WITHOUT FANGS
Zaki said the issue posed a major challenge as the integration of the NSM system had already been incorporated into the LCS construction process since the missile was selected in 2018.
[[nid:1434717]]
He said the ship's electronic systems, sensors and supporting hardware had been designed around the missile system.
"The ship's overall defence system is highly complex and interconnected.
"If the LCS is completed without its missile system, it would be like a tiger without fangs as the ship won't be able to operate as intended without one of its four-dimensional warfare capabilities."
However, he said the ships could still technically be completed under the "Fitted For But Not With" (FFBNW) concept, although operational effectiveness would be compromised.
Zaki said the issue was significant given the RMN's limited number of frontline strike assets.
"At present, the RMN only has six strike vessels comprising two Laksamana-class frigates, two Kasturi-class corvettes and two Perdana-class submarines.
"In the future, with the addition of three Littoral Mission Ships from (LMS) Batch 2 and five LCS vessels, the RMN should have 14 strike-capable assets.
"Although that figure is still relatively small compared with regional navies, it would still provide balance for a small force like the RMN."
He added that the navy was increasingly shifting  focus from quantity to quality by acquiring more modern equipment.
THE COST OF CHANGES
On operational readiness, Zaki said any delay or cancellation involving the NSM supply could also affect crew training and combat integration efforts ahead of the LCS commissioning.
He said  NSM was selected after a technical evaluation process against the French-made Exocet MM40 Block 3 missile system.
He added that Malaysia could still consider alternative anti-ship missile systems if the NSM procurement could not proceed.
Among the possible options, he said, were the Exocet MM40 Block 3 and Turkiye's Atmaca missile produced by Roketsan.
He said the Atmaca had already been selected as the anti-ship weapon system for the navy's  three LMS Batch 2 vessels currently being built in Turkiye.
"Based on the original Gowind design, the ship was indeed intended to operate with the latest-generation Exocet missile system.
"Malaysia was the only Gowind operator to select the NSM, while other users such as Egypt and the United Arab Emirates equipped their ships with Exocet missiles."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor resolves 99pc of Felda land title issues
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI: The Johor government has resolved 99.23 per cent of the long-standing land ownership issues involving Felda settlers statewide.
Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said addressing the decades-old problem, which had affected thousands of families, was a priority for the state administration.
Settlers had faced years of delays in land titles transfers due to administrative hurdles, inheritance disputes and unresolved compliance matters, leaving many without formal ownership for decades.
Speaking during his winding-up session at the state assembly in Bangunan Sultan Ismail yesterday, Onn Hafiz said 27,429 settlers received their land titles out of 27,642 applications submitted."
He said this in reply to issues raised by the Kahang assemblyman regarding Felda land titles and housing for future generations.
Onn Hafiz, who assumed office on March 15, 2022, following Barisan Nasional's victory in the state election, had pledged to deliver a lasting solution for the group.
"Johor views the matter as more than an administrative exercise. We understand that land ownership represents security, dignity and a legacy for Felda settlers and their future generations, he said.
[[nid:1434729]]
He added that Felda land was not merely an asset but symbolised the settlers' sacrifice and a livelihood to be passed down to their children and grandchildren.
Onn Hafiz said the state was also intensifying efforts to address home ownership for second and subsequent Felda generations  to ensure younger families were not left behind amid Johor's rapid development.
He said 10,663 applications, or about 86 per cent of the 12,392 applications received for such housing, had been approved.
"Of the total approved applications, 7,915 applicants received ownership titles, while the remaining 2,748 are undergoing the issuance process for Notice 5A."
Notice 5A under the National Land Code is a formal approval notice requiring the payment of land premiums and fees before a title is issued.
Onn Hafiz said the state would continue to coordinate with relevant agencies to ensure that the remaining applications were completed in accordance with existing procedures.
"The state government will continue to safeguard the settlers' welfare and that of future generations. We want them to enjoy the state's progress with stronger rights and a more secure future," he said.
For Felda settlers, Johor's approval of land ownership carries deep emotional significance after years of uncertainty over their status.
Many settlers described the development as a long-awaited assurance, offering greater peace of mind and financial security for their families, while also ensuring that future generations inherit legally recognised land and homes.
The move is also seen as strengthening confidence within Felda communities that their welfare and long-term interests remain  prioritised under the Johor government's rural development agenda.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US trade court rules against Trump's global 10 pct tariff
Article:
WASHINGTON: A US trade court on Thursday dealt President Donald Trump a fresh setback, ruling against the 10 per cent global tariffs he instituted after the Supreme Court struck down many earlier duties.
The 2-1 ruling by the US Court of International Trade, for now, blocks the tariffs from being implemented against just two companies and the state of Washington – but it could open doors to further such outcomes.
The decision found that the latest duty was not justified under the 1970s law cited in its implementation.
Trump imposed the temporary 10 per cent duty in February, shortly after the Supreme Court struck down many of his global tariffs.
The new tariff was meant to deal with balance of payments deficits, citing Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974.
It lasts only until late-July, unless extended by Congress, but the Trump administration has in the meantime been pursuing more lasting means to rebuild his trade agenda.
To do so, US officials have opened new investigations into dozens of trading partners over forced labour and overcapacity concerns – which could lead to fresh tariffs or other action.
The Court of International Trade ruling on Thursday ordered defendants to implement the decision within five days, and for the importers who sued in this case to receive refunds.
The Trump administration could appeal the trade court's decision.
"Section 122 was passed in response to a specific historical crisis that resulted in the United States' currency and gold reserves being depleted," said Liberty Justice Center senior counsel Jeffrey Schwab after the ruling.
"The United States has a trade deficit, not a balance-of-payments deficit, and does not have an international payments problem," Schwab said in a statement.
Trump's sector-specific tariffs on goods like steel, aluminium and autos remain unaffected by these legal challenges.
Yet Thursday's ruling marks the latest complication in Trump's tariffs agenda.
Since the high court dealt a sharp blow to Trump's economic policy, businesses have also rushed for refunds.
US Customs and Border Protection estimated in March that more than 330,000 importers could be eligible for refunds after the Supreme Court's decision.
The tariffs that were earlier struck down, imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, collected approximately US$166 billion in duties and estimated deposits.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: North Korea leader Kim inspects artillery that can hit South Korean capital
Article:
SEOUL: North Korean leader Kim Jong Un inspected the production of new artillery with a range exceeding 60km (37 miles) that would be deployed at the South Korean border and bolster its ability to hit Seoul and other targets, KCNA state news agency said on Friday.
Kim said the extended range of the new 155-mm self-propelled howitzer "will provide a great change and advantage in the land operations of our army," KCNA reported.
For decades, the North has been developing strategic weapons, including long-range ballistic missiles that could carry nuclear weapons and reach the US mainland.
In recent years it has also expanded its conventional arsenal.
North Korea's supply of missiles and artillery to Russia, in support of the war against Ukraine, has given it valuable battlefield data on its weapons, South Korean officials and experts have said.
Kim separately supervised the navigational manoeuvrability of one of its two modern navy destroyers being tested for deployment, KCNA said. Kim has ordered the construction of two additional destroyers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hackers block access to platform used by thousands of US schools
Article:
WASHINGTON: US universities including Harvard University, Stanford University and thousands of other institutions were hit by a massive cyber hack on Thursday following an earlier data breach.
The attack, claimed by ShinyHunters, a well-known cyber extortion group active since at least 2019, saw access to the Canvas learning platform blocked.
According to the Harvard Crimson student newspaper and posts on social media, students attempting to access the system on Thursday saw a message from the hacking group saying servers belonging to Canvas's parent company Instructure had "again" been breached.
"Instead of contacting us to resolve it they ignored us and did some 'security patches,'" the hackers said.
"If any of the schools in the affected list are interested in preventing the release of their data, please consult with a cyber advisory firm and contact us privately...to negotiate a settlement."
The group warned it would release all stolen data if schools did not make contact by May 12.
The message included a link to a list of schools ShinyHunters claims to have breached through Canvas.
Stanford University said Canvas was "currently unavailable due to an issue being experienced by the vendor," adding that Instructure had recently disclosed a nationwide information security issue it said had been contained.
But a further outage was now affecting Canvas customers including Stanford and "numerous other educational institutions nationwide," the university added.
Instructure said the stolen data in the original breach included personal details such as names, email addresses and student ID numbers, along with private messages exchanged between users.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Twin jihadist-claimed attacks kill more than 30 in Mali
Article:
BAMAKO: Two attacks in central Mali claimed by Al-Qaeda-linked fighters have killed more than 30 people, local, security and administrative sources told AFP on Thursday.
The two strikes came less than a fortnight after a large-scale, coordinated offensive by fighters and separatists on junta positions, which plunged the west African country into a fresh security crisis.
"At least 35 people were killed on Wednesday in near simultaneous attacks" on the villages of Korikori and Gomossogou, a youth official said.
A security and an administrative source both reported more than 30 dead in the assaults, claimed by the Group for the Support of Islam and Muslims (JNIM).
WAMAPS, a group of west African journalists specialising in Sahel security, said the provisional toll was more than 50 villagers killed and several still missing.
"Villages have been looted and some properties set on fire," the group added.
The security source said Thursday's assaults were in retaliation for acts committed by the Dan Nan Ambassagou militia, the best-known of the self-defence groups set up by local communities in response to the assaults plaguing central Mali.
"The victims are mostly militiamen. But there are also teenagers and children," the source told AFP.
Made up mainly of traditional ethnic Dogon hunters, Dan Nan Ambassagou has refused an order to disband by the authorities, who accused the militia of a massacre in the central village of Ogossagou that left 160 dead.
The Malian army said on Thursday it had carried out "a targeted operation against terrorist armed groups" in the area and around a dozen fighters were "neutralised".
It did not give further details.
In a statement on Thursday, the governor of the Bandiagara region "condemned these despicable and inhumane acts in the strongest possible terms."
Central Mali violence
The devastating assaults on April 25 and 26 by the JNIM and the Azawad Liberation Front (FLA), an ethnic Tuareg separatist movement, targeted strategic towns including Kidal in the desert north, and Kati, a garrison town near the capital Bamako.
Defence Minister Sadio Camara, the 47-year-old architect of Mali's military alliance with Russia, was killed by a car bomb at his residence.
Kidal and other towns and villages in the north were captured and are now under the control of the FLA and the fighters, who have since imposed a blockade on Bamako.
In recent years, central Mali has also been the theatre of deadly violence.
After the 2019 massacre, Ogossagou was the scene of a February 2020 raid that killed some 30 Fulanis, a nomadic people often accused across the Sahel of aiding fighters.
The UN has accused the Malian army and allied foreign fighters – likely Russian mercenaries from the Wagner paramilitary group – of executing at least 500 people in March 2022 during an anti-fighter operation in the town of Moura, a claim which the Malian junta denied.
And in June of that year, more than 130 civilians were killed in the town of Diallassagou in attacks attributed to JNIM fighters.
Wave of arrests, abductions
On Wednesday, security, legal and family sources told AFP that several opposition figures and military personnel had been detained or abducted following the large-scale attacks on the junta.
The military prosecutor's office said last week it had "solid evidence" of the "complicity" of certain members of the military, accusing them of helping with the "planning, coordination and execution" of the attacks.
But a political official said the wave of arrests and abductions smacked of a witch hunt.
"Everything suggests that these events are being used as an opportunity to carry out a purge within the political opposition and the army," the official told AFP, requesting anonymity for security reasons.
Since 2012, Mali has faced a deep security crisis fuelled in particular by violence from fighters affiliated with Al-Qaeda and the Islamic State group, as well as local criminal gangs and pro-independence groups.
On April 30, the JNIM called for a "common front" to "put an end to the junta" and usher in a peaceful and inclusive transition.
The country has been under military rule since back-to-back coups in 2020.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US, Gulf allies press UN to condemn Iran over Hormuz
Article:
UNITED NATIONS: The United States and its Gulf allies pressed the United Nations on Thursday to demand that Iran end its blockage of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, as a resolution on the issue faced a veto threat.
Diplomatic sources said veto-holding Russia, a key ally of Iran, had warned on Wednesday it was prepared to block the UN Security Council resolution.
US Ambassador Mike Waltz – speaking Thursday alongside counterparts from Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Qatar and Kuwait – argued the issue had global consequences.
"We believe in some basic fundamental principles, namely the freedom of navigation for the entire world's economies," Waltz said. "That's what's at stake here, nothing less than a cornerstone of worldwide stability and commerce."
A draft resolution from the United States and Bahrain, seen by AFP, demands that Iran halt its attacks and threats against commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.
It also orders Iran not to charge tolls on the passage, to specify where it has placed mines, and allow for the creation of a humanitarian corridor for shipments of fertiliser, amid fears of global food shortages.
[[nid:1434680]]
A fifth of the world's oil supplies and a third of global fertiliser shipments normally pass through the Strait of Hormuz, but shipping has been largely cut off since the war broke out on February 28 with US and Israeli attacks on Iran.
Iran's UN ambassador blasted the draft resolution as a "flawed, politically motivated" text that would not resolve the crisis.
Passing the resolution would "establish a dangerous precedent for legitimising unilateral coercive measures and unlawful actions by the United States against the sovereignty and sovereign rights of coastal states," said Amir Saeid Iravani.
In mid-March, the council passed a strongly worded resolution urging Iran to stop attacking its Gulf neighbours and condemning its chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz.
Russia and China abstained in that vote. But in early April they vetoed a text urging countries to coordinate their efforts in a "defensive" way to ensure free navigation through the strait.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: White House calls Mark Hamill 'sick' after actor posts Trump gravesite image
Article:
LOS ANGELES: The White House called "Star Wars" star Mark Hamill "one sick individual" on Thursday after an AI-generated image of US President Donald Trump in a shallow grave was posted on one of the actor's social media accounts.
"If Only" was inscribed on the image of Trump lying with his eyes closed adjacent to a gravestone, surrounded by daisies, with the inscription "Donald J. Trump 1946-2024." The image was posted on Hamill's verified Bluesky account.
Hamill, who played Luke Skywalker in the classic Star Wars films that debuted in 1977, wrote that Trump "should live long enough to witness his inevitable devastating loss in the midterms, be held accountable for his unprecedented corruption, impeached, convicted & humiliated for his countless crimes."
"Long enough to realise he'll be disgraced in the history books, forevermore," the actor added.
The White House responded on X, calling Hamill "one sick individual."
"These Radical Left lunatics just can't help themselves," a post from the White House press team said. "This kind of rhetoric is exactly what has inspired three assassination attempts in two years against our President."
Hamill later removed the gravestone image and sought to clarify his remarks.
"Actually, I was wishing him the opposite of dead, but apologise if you found the image inappropriate," he wrote on Bluesky.
Last month, a man stormed a security checkpoint and fired a shotgun outside the White House Correspondents' Association dinner in what authorities said was an attempt to assassinate Trump.
Trump was shot in the ear in 2024 at a rally in Pennsylvania. The shooter was killed by the Secret Service.
Also in 2024, a man was found hiding in bushes with weapons close to where Trump was golfing. He was convicted of attempted assassination in February.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US military says it carries out retaliatory strikes against Iran
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US military said it carried out retaliatory strikes on Iran on Thursday, targeting sites it said were responsible for attacking US forces in what it called unprovoked hostilities by Teheran.
Earlier, Iran's top joint military command said the US had violated a ceasefire by targeting an Iranian oil tanker and another ship entering the Strait of Hormuz, and by striking civilian areas.
"US Central Command (CENTCOM) eliminated inbound threats and targeted Iranian military facilities responsible for attacking US forces including missile and drone launch sites; command and control locations; and intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance nodes," the military said in a statement.
It added Iran had launched multiple missiles, drones and small boats as three US Navy destroyers, the Truxtun, Peralta and Mason, transited the Strait of Hormuz to the Gulf of Oman.
No US military assets were hit by the Iranians, the US military said.
"CENTCOM does not seek escalation but remains positioned and ready to protect American forces," the statement added.
It was not immediately clear what impact this would have on a ceasefire reached last month, but US Central Command described the strikes as being carried out in self-defence.
This is not the first time the two sides have exchanged fire since the ceasefire started.
On Monday, the US military said it destroyed six Iranian small boats and intercepted Iranian cruise missiles and drones as Teheran sought to thwart a US naval effort to open shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
Washington was still awaiting Iran's response to a US proposal that would stop the fighting but leave the most contentious issues, such as Iran's nuclear programme, unresolved for now.
The proposal would formally end the conflict in which full-scale warfare was paused by a ceasefire announced on April 7. But it does not address key US demands that Iran suspend its nuclear work and reopen the vital Strait of Hormuz, which before the war handled one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supply.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: City Hall moves to curb flash floods
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) is preparing short-, medium- and long-term measures to reduce flash floods in high-risk areas.
Mayor Datuk Seri Fadlun Mak Ujud said Wednesday's heavy rain, especially in Segambut and Cheras, caused rapid surface runoff from higher ground, overwhelming drains in low-lying areas.
"The drains could not cope. We have set aside funds this year to improve drainage, including in Cheras," he said.
Present were Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh, who is Segambut member of parliament, and Cheras MP Tan Kok Wai.
Fadlun said he had asked Tan to work with City Hall on measures to reduce pressure on flood-prone low-lying areas.
For Segambut, City Hall has identified a site for an on-site detention pond to temporarily hold rainwater before releasing it into rivers, easing pressure on drains during heavy rain.
Fadlun said City Hall had yet to receive precise rainfall intensity data from the Malaysian Meteorological Department, but the floods subsided soon after the rain stopped.
"This means the drainage system was functioning, but its capacity is strained during heavy rain, especially in low-lying areas that receive runoff from higher ground. These are the areas most affected by flash floods," he said.
He said City Hall is also tightening controls on hillside and slope developments.
This follows land clearing in areas such as Taman Tun Dr Ismail, which has increased surface runoff, causing rainwater to flow directly into drains instead of being absorbed by vegetation and soil.
He said City Hall would only allow development on slopes and hillsides to be carried out in phases, and not all at once.
He said City Hall would enforce the maintenance of ponds, silt fences and silt traps at construction sites to ensure water is properly contained before flowing into nearby areas.
Meanwhile, Yeoh said City Hall had begun making daily radio announcements on the weather from Wednesday, using MetMalaysia data.
She said the year-long initiative would help residents plan ahead, including avoiding parking in low-lying areas during expected heavy rain.
Wednesday's deluge triggered flash floods in several parts of the Klang Valley, including Kuala Lumpur and Petaling Jaya, causing traffic jams and leaving several vehicles stranded.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Husband's love carries a cancer survivor through haj
Article:
Bernama
Your browser does not support the audio element.
MAKKAH: Her steps are no longer steady, but 65-year-old Rossilawati Abdul Razak continues her journey in the Holy Land with remarkable perseverance, aided by slippers tied with string — a quiet symbol of her husband's enduring love.
For Rossilawati, the slippers are more than footwear; they have become a constant companion throughout her haj, despite long-standing health challenges.
Her journey took a difficult turn in Madinah when the strap of her specially adapted slippers snapped, making movement difficult due to her shorter right leg.
Her husband, Kamaruzaman Othman, 66, stepped in without hesitation.
He bought several pairs of slippers and modified them with string to suit her stride.
"He tied the slippers so I could walk. They have helped me move around," said Rossilawati from Seri Kembangan.
Behind the simple footwear lies a life of hardship for the former teacher, who once faced a life-threatening illness.
Rossilawati was diagnosed with Stage 4 rectal cancer in late 2014 and underwent 25 sessions of radiotherapy and chemotherapy, as the tumour was too large for surgery.
"Alhamdulillah… I am still here today and have been declared cancer-free," she said.
The illness left lasting effects after spreading to her thighbone, weakening it and causing mobility issues.
Radiotherapy also caused kidney complications, requiring a stent and the use of a colostomy bag.
Rossilawati's weight dropped from 80kg to 42kg — a period she describes as her most fragile.
Throughout her treatment, Kamaruzaman remained by her side, caring for and encouraging her.
"If it wasn't for my husband, I don't think I would still be here," she said.
Despite applying since 2016, the couple never expected to perform the haj together, given his wife's fragile health, Kamaruzaman said.
"When the medical results came out, we thought the chances were slim.
"We didn't tell anyone, fearing disappointment.
"But when Lembaga Tabung Haji asked for our passports and the Nusuk arrangements went smoothly, we felt this was truly Allah's invitation," he said.
He added that although Rossilawati uses a wheelchair for parts of the pilgrimage, they remain determined to complete the fifth pillar of Islam.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MFM capitalises on rising halal food demand
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Malayan Flour Mills Bhd (MFM) is capitalising on the rising halal food demand with its new ready-to-eat (RTE) products, as Malaysia strengthens its position as an international halal production hub.
MFM business development and government relations head Azhari Arshad said other countries are looking to Malaysia as a benchmark for halal standards, creating significant growth opportunities for domestic food manufacturers.
He said halal is no longer viewed solely from a compliance perspective, but is increasingly associated with food quality, safety and healthier consumption standards, driving stronger international demand.
"The halal industry is growing globally, not just in Malaysia. Countries are looking at us because we are considered the halal gold standard," he said after the launch of MFM's RTE expansion, which was held in conjunction with the company's 60th anniversary celebration.
He said the government's continued focus on strengthening Malaysia's halal ecosystem further supports the country's ambition to become a leading halal food production and export hub.
To capture growing demand, he said MFM is expanding its processing capacity while accelerating automation efforts across its operations to improve efficiency and long-term scalability.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Regent takes part in joint land, sea and air exercise with Johor Military Force and elite special forces unit
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, May 8 — His Royal Highness Tunku Ismail, The Regent of Johor, took part in a joint military exercise alongside the Johor Military Force (JMF) Elite Unit and the Malaysian Army’s elite 21 Grup Gerak Khas (GGK) yesterday.
The exercise involved coordinated land, sea and air operations based on the Riverine Ops and Heliborne Ops concepts.
GGK is the Malaysian Army’s premier special forces unit trained in counter-terrorism, jungle warfare, reconnaissance and special operations missions.
The joint drill showcased tactical coordination between the JMF Elite Unit and the military special operations force through simulated combat and rapid deployment exercises.

Title: KL Festival to become yearly event as govt pushes vibrant city life beyond work and malls
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 8 — The Kuala Lumpur Festival (KL Festival) is now a permanent fixture in the city’s yearly calendar in a bid to transform the city centre into a thriving cultural hub.
Federal Territories Minister Hannah Yeoh, who announced it last night, said that the KL Festival will be held annually starting from May next year, adding that it is also in support of the Visit Malaysia 2027 campaign and to further strengthen its status as a Unesco Creative City.
“For far too long, many people experienced Kuala Lumpur only as a city to work in, drive through, and rush home from. We want to change that.
“We want Kuala Lumpur to be a city people slow down to enjoy. A city that feels alive beyond office hours. A city where public spaces are activated with culture, creativity, and community.
“Because a city should not only feel alive inside shopping malls. Our streets, our heritage buildings, and public spaces must feel alive too,” Yeoh said during the launch.
Returning for its second edition this year, the KL Festival 2026 will run from May 6 to May 31 with over 80 programmes across 26 venues transforming heritage buildings, streets, and public spaces in Downtown KL into a network of cultural experiences.
This includes traditional arts like the wayang kulit reimagined, contemporary dance, immersive installations, outdoor cinema and theatre as well as community programmes.
Federal Territories Minister Hannah Yeoh speaks before the official launch of the Kuala Lumpur Festival on May 7, 2026. — Picture by  Raymond Manuel
As part of the Warisan KL national initiative, KL Festival is also focusing on heritage-led urban rejuvenation by encouraging people to rediscover urban spaces while fostering a deeper sense of belonging, collective pride and national identity.
“KL Festival is part of a long-term effort to strengthen Downtown Kuala Lumpur as a place people actively choose to spend time in — where culture, community and commerce come together to bring the city to life.
“It demonstrates how heritage and public spaces can be activated through curated programming, storytelling and partnerships, creating experiences that are meaningful and sustained over time.
“This is something we are building deliberately and systematically – working across the public, private and creative sectors to shape a more vibrant, liveable and culturally confident Kuala Lumpur,” said Think City’s managing director Datuk Hamdan Abdul Majeed.
KL Festival 2026 is organised by Think City along with the Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) with support from the Ministry of Finance and Federal Territories Department while Maxis is one of their main sponsors.
The festival, also held in conjunction with the Visit Malaysia Year 2026, opened yesterday evening with a gala performance coined as ‘Irama Pusaka – Akar Tradisi, Nadi Baharu’ at the DBKL Auditorium in Jalan Raja Laut.
Guests were treated to an evening musical feast featuring Orkestra Kuala Lumpur alongside leading ensembles including Gangsapura (gamelan), Lee Rubber Chinese Orchestra, EKAM Ensemble, and sape virtuoso Leslie Eli.
Communication Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil, Transport Minister Anthony Loke, and KL Mayor Datuk Fadlun Mak Ujud were also present at the KL Festival 2026 opening night.
The evening was also graced by the appearance of several other prominent Malaysians such as former national squash player Datuk Nicol David, singer and musician Loque as well as theatre doyenne Jo Kukathas.
More information on the KL Festival 2026 is available
here
.

Title: Why Klang Valley still looks busy despite tighter consumer spending
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 8 — Parking bays at major malls across the Klang Valley continue filling up even on weekdays, with cars circling for spots.
Inside these malls, shoppers remain noticeable with queues visible outside popular restaurants with families gathered at eateries and cafés that stay lively well into the evening.
But beneath the business-as-usual facade, questions remain how and why the situation is such despite advice from the government for more prudent spending.
At malls such as Sunway Pyramid, Mid Valley Megamall and MyTown Cheras, the crowds have not disappeared.
However, some are saying it no longer feels like the free-spending retail boom seen in previous years.
Instead of impulse shopping, consumers are planning purchases around promotions, prioritising essentials and cutting back on discretionary spending.
“People are still coming out, but they think longer before buying.
“They browse, compare prices and then wait for sales,” said a retail worker in MyTown Cheras.
MyTown Cheras patrons on a Tuesday afternoon. — Picture by Soo Wern Jun
A space for everything
For many urban families, malls remain one of the few convenient places where everything is available in a single location.
Lee Lee Tham, 35, a mother of two, said visiting the mall has become a weekly outing that her family rarely skips.
“There is everything at the mall, so it’s the most convenient place for us to gather.
